Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent this semester. I'd usually try to listen to lecture recordings while commuting or doing other things since I learn better by hearing stuff. I'd set aside time on weekends to catch up, but between my extracurricular activities and everything else going on, those plans often fell apart. I'd end up cramming in study sessions whenever I could find a gap, which wasn't ideal but it's how I survived.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be really on top of things, studying consistently for maybe 18 hours spread across the week. Other weeks, especially when my extracurriculars got intense, I'd barely touch the course materials until a deadline forced my hand. I tried to be regular but life just didn't cooperate most of the time.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes my peers in the discussion groups would mention something that made me think I should check in. I'd also log in when I remembered I hadn't been on in a while and felt a bit guilty about it. The peer support connections I made actually helped remind me to stay somewhat engaged.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where logging in became more routine for me. I'd joined a study group through the peer support program, and we'd coordinate through the LMS discussions. For those few weeks, checking the platform became part of my daily habit because I wanted to see what my peers were saying. But once that group got busy with their own stuff, I fell out of the routine pretty quickly.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me because I could listen to them multiple times. As an auditory learner, hearing the professor explain concepts stuck with me way better than reading. The discussion forums were also helpful when people actually engaged – hearing different perspectives through written conversations helped me process ideas. I watched nearly 300 minutes of video content because that format just works for my brain.
Q6: I often skipped over the reading materials and some of the supplementary documents. They just didn't click for me the way audio content did. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd sit unopened on my laptop. The practice quizzes I also delayed a lot because they felt tedious, even though I knew they'd probably help. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to what I needed to know for exams or assignments. If I could listen to it or discuss it with someone, even better. The peer interactions made things feel more valuable because I wasn't just learning alone. When something felt disconnected from the actual assessments or too abstract, I'd lose interest quickly.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different – they were the first things I'd cut when time got tight. Required stuff had a deadline attached, so it created urgency. Optional materials felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Even when I knew they might help, the lack of immediate consequences made them easy to skip. I'd tell myself I'd come back to them but rarely did.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that the LMS wouldn't capture. I'd download the lecture audio and listen on my phone while working out or on the bus. I also used some YouTube videos and podcasts that explained similar concepts in different ways. My peer study group would meet up in person sometimes, and we'd work through problems together using our notes. That collaborative learning was huge for me.
Q10: There were many times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd replay downloaded lectures repeatedly to really understand something – that wouldn't register. Or when I'd spend hours discussing concepts with classmates over coffee, working through problems on paper. I also did a lot of thinking and processing while doing other activities, mentally reviewing what I'd heard in lectures.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then download it or the audio and revisit it multiple times offline. The same with lecture slides – I'd pull them up once online, then study from my downloaded copy for days. My actual engagement with those materials was way higher than what the single access would suggest. It was just more convenient to study from my device without needing internet.
Q12: There was definitely a rough patch around midterms when my extracurricular commitments peaked at the same time. I was surprised by how quickly I fell behind – it felt like one week I was managing okay, and the next I was completely overwhelmed. My attendance dropped because I was physically exhausted and couldn't make it to early classes. It wasn't that I stopped caring; I just couldn't keep all the balls in the air.
Q13: The workload from other courses and my extracurriculars definitely impacted how I used the LMS. When I was exhausted, I'd log in but just kind of scroll without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules because I didn't know where to start. I'd focus on whatever felt most manageable rather than what was most important, which probably wasn't the smartest strategy.
Q14: Oh definitely, there were times I'd have the LMS open while doing other things, technically logged in but not really there mentally. I'd start a video and realize ten minutes later I hadn't heard a word because my mind wandered. Sometimes I'd click through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't actually learning. It was like going through the motions without the substance.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was that my extracurriculars felt more immediately rewarding – I could see the impact of my efforts there right away. With coursework, the payoff felt distant and uncertain. I kept thinking I'd have time later, but later never came with enough hours.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active – watching videos, checking resources, posting in discussions. Without that pressure, I'd drift away from the platform. It's not a great way to learn, I know, but that urgency was often the only thing that could cut through everything else competing for my attention.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got encouraging feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But when feedback was critical or I did poorly, I felt surprised and a bit discouraged, which sometimes made me avoid that area for a while. The peer support helped buffer some of that – my study partners would help me process negative feedback and figure out how to improve.
Q18: The course structure was a bit challenging for me because it seemed designed for people who could attend regularly and keep up week by week. With my attendance being pretty low, I often felt like I was playing catch-up. The way content built on previous weeks meant that falling behind created a snowball effect. I wished there was more flexibility for people with irregular schedules.
Q19: The video content being available for replay made engagement easier for me – that was crucial for my learning style. But the heavy reliance on written materials and readings made it harder. The discussion forums were helpful when they were active, but sometimes they felt dead, which was discouraging. Having peer support connections made a real difference in keeping me somewhat engaged when I might have completely checked out.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to produce quality work. They might be watching videos, reading materials, exploring the LMS, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. Or maybe they're dealing with personal stuff that lets them passively consume content but not actively create. The activity shows effort even if the output doesn't reflect it.
Q21: That somewhat reflects my experience, actually. I spent a lot of time learning outside the LMS – listening to downloaded content, studying with peers, using external resources. My LMS activity looks pretty low, but I was putting in effort, just not in ways the system could track. Unfortunately, my exam score shows that maybe my methods weren't as effective as I thought, but I was definitely engaged beyond what the logs show.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how long you spend on videos, whether you meet deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement – whether you're actually absorbing material or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, peer discussions, or the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They show behavior but not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just checking boxes. It's when you're curious about the content, discussing it with others, thinking about it even when you're not studying. For me, engagement is when I'm actively listening and trying to connect new information to what I already know. It's more about mental investment than just time spent logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to stay motivated and work through difficult concepts. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials or the discussions with classmates outside the platform. It also wouldn't show that despite my poor performance, I actually cared about the course and was trying to climb back up. My resilience and effort don't translate into login data.
